fragments.content: Lung cancer is the leading cause of cancer death worldwide. Two main types: non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC, 85% of cases) and small cell lung cancer (SCLC, 15%). NSCLC subtypes: adenocarcinoma (most common, often peripheral, associated with EGFR/ALK mutations), squamous cell carcinoma (central, smoking-related, P40/P63 positive), large cell carcinoma (diagnosis of exclusion). SCLC characterized by rapid growth, early metastasis, neuroendocrine features (chromogranin, synaptophysin positive), initially chemo-sensitive but develops resistance. Molecular drivers in NSCLC: EGFR mutations (10-15% Western, 50% Asian, more in never-smokers, women, adenocarcinoma); ALK rearrangements (3-5%); ROS1 (1-2%); BRAF V600E (2-4%); KRAS (25-30%, historically undruggable, now sotorasib/adagrasib available); MET exon 14 skipping (3-4%); RET fusions (1-2%); NTRK fusions (<1%). PD-L1 expression guides immunotherapy use.

NSCLC treatment depends on stage and molecular profile. Early stage (I-II): surgical resection (lobectomy preferred); adjuvant chemotherapy for stage II-III; adjuvant osimertinib for EGFR-mutated (ADAURA trial); adjuvant immunotherapy (atezolizumab/pembrolizumab) for PD-L1+ after chemo. Locally advanced (stage III): concurrent chemoradiation followed by durvalumab consolidation (PACIFIC trial showed significant OS benefit). Metastatic (stage IV): molecular testing mandatory before treatment. EGFR-mutated: osimertinib first-line (FLAURA, median OS 38.6 months). ALK-positive: alectinib or lorlatinib first-line. ROS1-positive: entrectinib or crizotinib. No driver mutation: immunotherapy ± chemotherapy based on PD-L1. PD-L1 ≥50%: pembrolizumab monotherapy (KEYNOTE-024). PD-L1 1-49%: pembrolizumab + chemotherapy (KEYNOTE-189 for non-squamous, KEYNOTE-407 for squamous). PD-L1 <1%: immunotherapy + chemotherapy or nivolumab + ipilimumab + chemotherapy (CheckMate 9LA). SCLC: limited stage - concurrent chemoradiation (etoposide + cisplatin/carboplatin); extensive stage - chemo + immunotherapy (atezolizumab/durvalumab + platinum/etoposide).

Lung cancer diagnosis requires tissue confirmation and comprehensive staging. Initial evaluation: chest CT with contrast, PET-CT for staging (detects distant metastases), brain MRI (especially for adenocarcinoma, SCLC, stage III+). Tissue acquisition: bronchoscopy with biopsy for central lesions; CT-guided needle biopsy for peripheral lesions; EBUS-TBNA for mediastinal lymph node sampling; thoracentesis if pleural effusion present. Pathology: histologic subtype classification; immunohistochemistry (TTF-1 for adenocarcinoma, P40/P63 for squamous); neuroendocrine markers for SCLC. Molecular testing: NGS panel preferred to detect multiple alterations simultaneously; liquid biopsy (ctDNA) if tissue inadequate or for monitoring. Staging: TNM system 8th edition; SCLC traditionally staged as limited (confined to one radiation field) vs extensive. Pulmonary function tests assess surgical candidacy. Performance status (ECOG) guides treatment tolerance. Surveillance post-treatment: CT chest every 6 months for 2 years, then annually.
